coredata.dc:title: In-situ nitrogen-doped carbon dots for fluorescence sensing of tetracycline antibiotic
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l.
coredata.dc:creator: Yuan, Mengdi
An, Jia
Zhang, Guoxiu
Hu, Yongqin
Luo, Mengfei
Shi, Yuxia
Liu, Yufei
coredata.dc:description: Tetracycline (TET) is one of the common antibiotics used in animal husbandry. However, the long-term consumption of TET may increase the risks for human health, especial in dairy products. Moreover, after entering the human body, TET is difficult to be absorbed by the intestines and stomach, and will be discharged into the sewage in the form of the parent compound, which will cause irreversible damage to the natural environment. Novel nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) nanoparticles were successfully fabricated using in situ hydrothermal reaction on the inner surface of the aminated central radial silica spheres (CRSSs-NH2), called CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs. The aminated central radial carrier played a role in protecting and stabilizing N-CDs, thus enhancing it fluorescence and realizing solid-state light emission. The results shown that the optimal excitation wavelength of CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs was about 365 nm, and the emission wavelength was about 446 nm. The CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs was then used as a fluorescent probe for rapidly detecting TET. With optimized experimental conditions, the fluorescence quenching intensity of CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs had a good linear relationship with the concentration of TET. The linear detection range was 0.5–60 μM, and the limit of detection is 0.39 μM. Furthermore, the TET-sensing application in milk has been carried out, and the average recovery range is 91.90%–104.70%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Electrostatic adsorption
Central radial silica spheres
Tetracycline detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM and TEM images of CRSSs (a, b), TEM and TEM mapping images of CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs (c,d). The FT-IR spectra (e) of CRSSs and CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs, and N2 sorption/desorption isotherms (f) of CRSSs, CRSSs-NH2 and CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs.

The change of (a) fluorescent intensity of CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs (prepared at different heating time) as a function of molar ratio of DETA: CA. (b) the UV absorption spectrum, excitation wavelength and emission wavelength of CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs. (c) the emission peaks of CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs scanned at different excitation wavelengths. (d) CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs realizes solid-state light emission.
The influence of (a) storage time, (b) pH, (c) ionic strength on fluorescence intensity of CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs. (d) Research on the proper storage temperature of CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs.

(a) The UV absorption spectra of TET, excitation spectra and Emission spectra of CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs. (b) Absorption spectra of TET, CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs and TET. (c) the ultraviolet absorption spectrum curves of CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs with or without the addition of TET.

(a) The relative fluorescence intensity values of CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs containing 50 mM different substances when there is no TET (red bar) or 1 mM TET (blue bar). (b) Fluorescence spectra of CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs in the presence of different concentrations of TET (0–1000 μM). (c) When the TET concentration is in the range of 0–1000 μM, the relationship between its concentration and (I0–I)/I0 (the illustration shows the linear relationship between the TET concentration of 0.5–60 μM and (I0–I)/I0). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Synthetic schematic diagram of CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs (Inspired by sea urchins) and TET sensing detection.

head.ce:title: In-situ nitrogen-doped carbon dots for fluorescence sensing of tetracycline antibiotic

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Technology & Systems (Chongqing University), Ministry of Education, Chongqing, 400044, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Technology & Systems (Chongqing University)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre for Intelligent Sensing Technology (CIST), College of OptoElectronic Engineering, Chongqing University, Chongqing, 400044, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Centre for Intelligent Sensing Technology (CIST), College of OptoElectronic Engineering, Chongqing University, Chongqing 400044, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre for Nano Health, College of Science, Swansea University, Singleton Park, Swansea, SA2 8PP, UK

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Technology & Systems (Chongqing University), Ministry of Education, Chongqing, 400044, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Technology & Systems (Chongqing University)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tetracycline (TET) is one of the common antibiotics used in animal husbandry. However, the long-term consumption of TET may increase the risks for human health, especial in dairy products. Moreover, after entering the human body, TET is difficult to be absorbed by the intestines and stomach, and will be discharged into the sewage in the form of the parent compound, which will cause irreversible damage to the natural environment. Novel nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) nanoparticles were successfully fabricated using in situ hydrothermal reaction on the inner surface of the aminated central radial silica spheres (CRSSs-NH2), called CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs. The aminated central radial carrier played a role in protecting and stabilizing N-CDs, thus enhancing it fluorescence and realizing solid-state light emission. The results shown that the optimal excitation wavelength of CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs was about 365 nm, and the emission wavelength was about 446 nm. The CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs was then used as a fluorescent probe for rapidly detecting TET. With optimized experimental conditions, the fluorescence quenching intensity of CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs had a good linear relationship with the concentration of TET. The linear detection range was 0.5–60 μM, and the limit of detection is 0.39 μM. Furthermore, the TET-sensing application in milk has been carried out, and the average recovery range is 91.90%–104.70%.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracycline (TET) is a broad-spectrum bacteriostatic agent that has a bactericidal effect at high concentrations [1]. The bactericidal mechanism is mainly to prevent the combination of aminoacyl and ribonucleoprotein bodies, and prevent the growth of peptide chains and protein synthesis [2]. As a consequence, TET is often used as a therapeutic drug and feed additive for the anti-inflammatory treatment of dairy cows [3]. If long term abused, it may cause residual tetracycline in the milk, which will threat to human health, such as causing liver damage [4], tetracycline teeth [5]. Similarly, it's difficult for the human body to absorb TET, TET will be discharged into the sewage in the form of the parent compound. However, it's difficult for the environment to completely degrade it, and it is likely to become a source of environmental and ecological hazards. On the other hand, it is a great challenge and necessary to monitor the residues of TET. Up to now, the existing detection methods for TET mainly include high - performance liquid chromatography [6,7], capillary electrophoresis [8], and microbiological methods [9]. High performance liquid chromatography equipment is expensive and complicated to operate, which limits the application of real-time testing. Furthermore, enzyme-linked immunoassay is time-consuming and has poor repeatability. Therefore, it is of great significance to human health to construct a rapid, sensitive and simple detection method for TET.

Recent studies have found that carbon dots (CDs), as electron donors or acceptors, have excellent chemiluminescence properties. CDs are a relatively new type of quantum dots, which have the advantages of good stability, low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility. The sensors based on CDs are based on the interaction of internal filtering effect [10], ion binding reaction [11], and charge transfer [12], which causes the change of fluorescence intensity that to achieve target detection. The fluorescence analysis technology has received more and more attentions due to its advantages such as high sensitivity, good stability, short response time, simple operation and low cost. It is widely used in heavy metal detection [13,14], nitro aromatic explosives detection [15,16], vitamin and drug detection [17,18]. But it also has the disadvantages of too small particle size, easy self-aggregation and quenching, and difficult to capture, so it is very necessary to find an excellent carrier.

Sea urchins are invertebrates that live in shallow waters of the ocean. Their main characteristics are spherical or disc-shaped. The spines on their bodies have super anti-interference performance, which can not only maintain their cleanliness, but also resist external invasions. Central radial silica spheres (CRSSs) are an excellent supported nano-carrier similar to sea urchins. They have not only large three-dimensional pores comparable to sea urchin spines, but also easy to modify grafts and have a highly accessible inner surface. They have been widely used in the fields of drug delivery [19], chromatographic analysis [20], and biological imaging [21]. Dong et al. grew carbon dots in situ in mesoporous silica spheres to achieve the detection of Fe3+ [22]. Wang et al. doped carbon dots into aminated mesoporous silica spheres, and super sensitively detected the adsorption efficiency to the uranium [23].

The purpose of this research is to fabricate a fluorescence sensor based on aminated CRSSs that composited by nitrogen-doped carbon dots (CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs), and to explore its detection application of TET in milk. Inspired by sea urchin spines, the aminated CRSSs (CRSSs-NH2) has a central radial structure that resembles a sea urchin, perfectly avoided the fluorescence self-quenching of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) as the aggregation, and realized the solid state fluorescence. The morphology, stability and optical properties of the synthesized CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs were studied and used to detect the TET. The study found that the quenching effect of TET on CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs is obvious, when the TET concentration is in the range of 0.5–60 μM, it has a perfect linear relationship with the fluorescence quenching efficiency of CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs, the minimum detection limit is 0.39 μM, the average recovery range of TET is 91.90%–104.70%.

Triethylamine (TEA), cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), and diethylenetriamine (DETA) were all purchased from Chengdu Clone Chemicals Co., Ltd. Tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) was purchased from Sigma. Sodium salicylate was purchased from Comeo Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. 3-Aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) and tetracycline hydrochloride were purchased from Aladdin. The citric acid (CA) was purchased from Shanghai Hushi Chemical Co., Ltd. All chemical reagents are analytical standards.

2 g of CA and 1.02 mL of DETA sonicate dissolved in 20 mL of pure water. The mixture was subjected to a high temperature hydrothermal treatment at 200 °C for 6 h, then let it cool down naturally to room temperature, and dialyzed about 72 h with a molecular cutoff of 1 kDa, and then dried with a rotary evaporator to obtain a yellow solid, which was placed at 4 °C for next used.

Dissolved 60 mg of TEA in 20 mL of pure water, sonicated for 10 min, and stirred continuously for 30 min at a temperature of 70 °C, added 0.16 g of sodium salicylate and 0.38 g of CTAB, and continue to stir for 1 h. Then added 1 mL TEOS every 2 min for a total of 4 mL, and continue to gently stir for 4 h. After washing with pure water and ethanol for several times, dispersed in HCl/ethanol mixture, stirred at 60 °C for 4 h, repeated extraction three times, washed thoroughly with ethanol and pure water, and dry with rotary evaporator to obtain white. The powder should be stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C.

Dissolved 0.5 g of CRSSs in 6.25 mL of 10% APTES, added 500 μL of ammonia, and shaked for 12 h at room temperature. After washed with ethanol and pure water, the precipitate was dissolved in 50 mL of pure water, and 5 g of CA and 10 mL of DETA were added. After ultrasonic for half an hour, it was transferred to an autoclave for hydrothermal reaction at 200 °C for 6 h. After the reaction is completed, it is naturally cooled to room temperature, and then thoroughly washed with ethanol and pure water until the supernatant is free of fluorescence, and then placed in an oven at 60 °C for drying.

Dissolved TET in a Britton-Robison (B-R) buffer solution of pH = 7.0 with a final concentration of 50 mM. Dispersed CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs in a certain amount of B-R buffer solution pH = 7.0 with a concentration of 0.5 mg/mL. Pipetted a small amount of CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs dispersion, and added different concentrations of TET solution. The concentration range of TET was 0–1000 μM. Then put it into the fluorescence spectrometer, use I to represent the fluorescence intensity value at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm, and then under the same conditions, use I0 to represent the fluorescence intensity value of the blank sample (without TET). Recorded and saved the data, and each concentration was measured three times.

In order to determine the specific detection of TET by CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs, penicillin (PNC), cephalosporin (CPS), Erythromycin (EM), Lincomycin (MY), Ofloxacin (EV), Amoxicillin (AML), glucose (Glu), sucrose (SUC), vitamin c (VC), citric acid (CA), cysteine (Cys), tyrosine (Tyr), Glycine (Gly), Al3+, Ca2+, K+, Mg2+, Na+, and Zn2+ were detected at the concentration of 1 mM TET and the concentration of 50 mM possible interference substances in milk.

The microscopic morphology of the material was revealed by the field emission scanning electron microscope (SU8020) produced by Hitachi (Japan), and Thermal Fisher Scientific (Netherlands) ‘s field emission transmission electron microscope (Talos F200S). The infrared spectrum was measured by the Nicolet IS10 infrared spectrometer produced by Nicolet in the United States. N2 isothermal adsorption was tested by the physical adsorption instrument (ASAP 2460) produced by Mike Company in the United States. The UV absorption curve was measured under the Shimadzu UV-2600 spectrometer. The Zeta potential data was tested by Zetasizer Ultra produced by Malvern Panakko in England. The absolute fluorescence quantum yield is measured by FluoroMax-4 produced by Horiba (Japan). Fluorescence spectrum was measured by Hitachi F-4700 fluorescence spectrometer.

As shown in Scheme 1 , this study first used ethyl orthosilicate as the silicon source, cetyltrimethylammonium bromide as the cationic template, sodium salicylate as the anionic template, and triethylamine as the catalyst. The CRSSs with large three-dimensional pores and easy graft modification on the surface was prepared. Then the amino group was used as an adsorbent to modify the spheres, the N-CDs were dispersed stably and uniformly by electrostatic adsorption. As shown in Fig. S1, the negative potential CRSSs-NH2 firmly adsorbs the positive potential N-CDs, proved that the amino group was successfully adsorbed on the ball.

With its excellent performance, the CRSSs-NH2 successfully solved the problem of fluorescence quenching caused by excessive local concentration of N-CDs. It not only firmly captured N-CDs, but also realized the solid-state fluorescence emission of N-CDs, which enhanced Fluorescence of N-CDs. After investigation, the existence of TET can quench the fluorescence of CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs, which can realize the selective sensing of TET.

First, the structures of CRSSs were characterized by TEM and SEM. The TEM images of N-CDs were shown in Fig. S2, It can be seen that its average particle size is only 5 nm. It can be seen from the SEM image of Fig. 1 a that the sphere is a clear central radial pore structure, and it has good dispersion. The uniformly dispersed spheres can also be observed from the TEM image of Fig. 1b, and the average particle size is about 224 nm by statistics of the particle size. The center-radial structure of the silica spheres is due to the adsorption of anions and cationic template agents that make the anions enter the hydrophobic region of the cationic micelle [24]. Fig. 1c is the TEM characterization of CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs, which basically maintains the morphology of the sphere. The introduction of N-CDs has caused some degree of etching in the morphology of the CRSSs, which may be due to the precursor of N-CDs are alkaline [25]. The average particle size is about 140 nm by statistics of its particle size. Fig. 1d is the TEM mapping of CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs. It is obvious that there are four elements: carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and silicon in this sample, it further illustrates the successful introduction of N-CDs.

Infrared image of the N-CDs is shown in Fig. S3, prove that N-CDs are rich in carboxyl and amino groups. Fig. 1e shows the infrared images of CRSSs and CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs. The peaks at 800 cm−1 and 466 cm−1 (Si–O symmetric tensile vibration absorption), the peak at 960 cm−1 (flexural vibration absorption of Si–OH), and the strong and wide absorption band appears at about 1088 cm−1 (anti-symmetric tensile vibration absorption of Si–O–Si) are all confirming that the CRSSs is silica [22]. After the N-CDs are encapsulated in CRSSs-NH2, the infrared spectrum still has the characteristic peaks of silica. The broad absorption peak at 3433 cm−1 is caused by the stretching vibration of the O–H of water and the N–H (APTES). The peaks at 2952 cm−1 and 1465 cm−1 (C–H bond), the peak at 1787 cm−1 (CO bond) are proved that N-CDs were successfully encapsulated in CRSSs-NH2. So that, the surface of CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs sample contains a lot of hydroxyl, carboxyl and other oxygen-containing groups, which proves that the excellent hydrophilic property consists in CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs.

Fig. 1f shows the N2 isothermal adsorption and desorption curves of CRSSs, CRSSs-NH2, and CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs. CRSSs-NH2 presents a typical type Ⅳ adsorption and desorption isotherm, with obvious hysteresis loops in the region of P/P0 = 0.5–1.0. After loading the N-CDs, the area of the hysteresis ring, the number of mesopores and the specific surface area decreased (Table S1), indicating that the N-CDs were successfully filled into the pores of CRSSs-NH2. All of the above prove that the N-CDs are successfully doped in the pores of the CRSSs-NH2.

The experimental results show that the fluorescence performance of CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs is obviously affected by the molar ratio of DETA to CA. Fig. 2 a shows the fluorescence intensity of CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs prepared under different conditions. With the extension of the hydrothermal reaction time, the fluorescence intensity has no obvious change. However, the amount of DETA has a significant effect on strength. As the molar ratio of DETA to CA increases, the fluorescence intensity tends to increase significantly. Because –OH, –COOH and –NH2 groups help intermolecular dehydration and carbonization, they are very important for the formation of carbon dots. If the reactants only contain –OH and –COOH groups, the quantum yield (QY) obtained will not be very high. However, if there are amino groups, the QY value will increase significantly, resulting in an increase in the fluorescence intensity of CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs. Therefore, increasing the proportion of DETA is beneficial to increase the QY. When the molar ratio of DETA to CA is greater than 3:1, the polymer carbon dots in the transition period are not easy to continue the carbonization reaction, which will cause the reaction system to be too alkaline, and the CRSSs will be further corroded. Therefore, we choose the molar ratio of DETA to CA to be 2:1, and the hydrothermal reaction time to be 6 h, in order to carry out the experiment.

Fig. 2b shows the UV absorption spectrum, excitation wavelength and emission wavelength of CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs. The inset is the comparison of CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs under natural light and 365 nm excitation light. It can be seen that CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs (0.5 mg/mL) is excited at 365 nm, and it has significant blue fluorescence at the wavelength. The ultraviolet absorption peaks of N-CDs are at 245 nm and 365 nm, respectively. The absorption peak at 245 nm is caused by the π-π* transition of the CC sp2 structure, and is not the cause of the fluorescence signal. The absorption peak near 365 nm belongs to the n-π* transition of CO, which is the cause of the strong emission peak at 446 nm (Fig. S4). Two faint peaks were also found in the UV absorption spectrum of CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs, indicating that N-CDs were successfully synthesized in CRSSs-NH2, and the fluorescence spectrum properties of CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs were consistent with N-CDs. In addition, CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs has the same “excitation independence” as N-CDs, and the emission peaks of CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs and N-CDs are scanned at different excitation wavelengths did not undergo significant red shift or blue shift (Fig. 2c).

Characterized by a stable transient fluorescence instrument, its absolute the QY value can reach 49.1% (Fig. S5), the absolute QY value of N-CDs is about 42%. and the fluorescence lifetime of CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs is almost the same as that of N-CDs (Fig. S6), indicating that CRSSs are good three-dimensional carriers and the amino groups embedded in CRSSs maximize the stability of N-CDs in the three-dimensional network of pores. As shown in Fig. 2d, CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs also avoids the quenching of N-CDs caused by self-aggregation, and realizes solid-state light emission.

As shown in Fig. 3 a, the relative photoluminescence intensity change of the composite material hardly fluctuates within 50 days. This phenomenon fully shows that the prepared CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs composite material has excellent fluorescence stability.

The detection of TET in the actual environment or biological samples is complicated as the detection may be affected by a variety of factors, completing with pH, ionic strength, and temperature. Fig. 3b shows the change of the fluorescence intensity of CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs with pH of 2.0–12.0 in the B-R buffer system. On the whole, CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs has good stability to the pH of the environment, the follow-up experiments of detecting TET use B-R buffer with pH = 7.0. In Fig. 3c, we recorded the fluorescence intensity of the CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs composite under different sodium chloride concentrations of pH = 7.0, and found that the fluorescence intensity was very stable, indicating that high ionic intensity had no effect on the fluorescence signal of the composite. From Fig. 3d, we can know that the best storage temperature of the material should not be higher than 20 °C. The three-dimensional large pores of CRSSs can effectively protect the nanoparticles, so that the composite material has super stability and will not be affected by the surrounding environment.

The residue of TET has brought huge hidden dangers to human health and the ecological environment. Research has found that tetracycline has an obvious quenching effect on the prepared CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the excitation spectrum of CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs is at 365 nm, TET has an absorption in this range, and there is a certain degree of spectral overlap, which meets the conditions for fluorescence internal filtration. Observed from the UV absorption spectrum in Fig. 4b, TET has an absorption peak at 215 nm, 274 nm and 360 nm. After interacting with CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs, the peak at around 215 nm and the peak at around 274 nm are red-shifted, which may be TET. The molecule and the carboxyl or amino group in CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs form a ground state complex through hydrogen bonding, and the quenching process may be static quenching. From the fluorescence lifetime results in Fig. 4c, after adding TET to CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs, there is almost no change in the fluorescence intensity decay rate, which further shows that the quenching method is static quenching. Therefore, it can be considered that the fluorescence quenching of CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs by TET is mainly the result of the synergistic effect of internal filtering effect and static quenching.

In addition, milk contains many kinds of substances, which may contain other elements that may affect the fluorescence signal of CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs. Therefore, it's necessary to prove the specific quenching of CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs fluorescence by TET. As shown in Fig. 5 a, dissolve the same concentration of specific interfering substances in the same amount of CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs, Measure the fluorescence intensity. Then add TET in it, Record the fluorescence intensity again. So the fluorescence data with and without TET are obtained, and draw a plot of relative fluorescence intensity. It can be observed intuitively that a small amount of TET will cause the fluorescence of CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs to be greatly quenched, while the presence of other interfering substances at 50 times the concentration of TET has no obvious effect on the fluorescence of the composite material.

For the detection conditions of TET, we selected from the previous experimental results in B-R buffer with pH = 7.0. As shown in Fig. 5b, In the range of TET concentration of 0–1000 μM, it can be clearly seen that the fluorescence signal of CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs gradually decreases with the increase of TET concentration. As shown in Fig. 5c, I0 represents the fluorescence intensity value of the blank sample (without TET) at the 446 nm emission peak, similarly I represents the fluorescence intensity value after adding different concentrations of TET. the concentration of TET (μM) in the solution is represented by C. The linear regression equation is (I0–I)/I0 = 0.00279C + 0.02, and the correlation coefficient is R2 = 0.994. Therefore, in the range of TET concentration of 0.5–60 μM, the CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs shows an excellent linear correlation with it, and the limit of detection (LOD) is 0.39 μM. As a control, if only N-CDs are used to detect TET (Fig. S7), the linear range obtained is 2–40 μM, and the calculated LOD is 0.78 μM, the effect is not satisfactory. Therefore, using CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs to detect TET is the best choice. Compared with other methods for detecting TET, this work uses a new type of fluorescence sensor to detect TET with similar excellent results (Table S2). In summary, the CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs composite material can be used as a great TET detection sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TET in milk samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to verify the feasibility of the fluorescence detection method, in-situ nitrogen-doped carbon dots have been used for the detection of TET in milk samples, the results of which are listed in Table 1 . The results show that the average recovery rate is 91.90%–104.70%, while the relative standard deviations do not exceed 6.6%, which demonstrated that the proposed method for the determination of TET is reliable and can be used for the determination of actual samples.

In summary, the novel developed N-CDs was stably and efficiently encapsulated into the three-dimensional pores of aminated central radial silica spheres using a hydrothermal in-situ growth method. CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs were characterized and analyzed using TEM, FT-IR, UV–vis and N2 sorption, which confirmed that N-CDs have been successfully synthesized in CRSSs-NH2, showing the good fluorescence characteristics and high stability. CRSSs-NH2@N-CDs was then studied for TET fluorescence sensing. Based on the good linear relationship between the relative fluorescence intensity and the TET concentration, fluorescent probe was developed and successfully applied for the detecting TET in milk, with the advantages of less time-consuming, lower cost and higher sensitivity. This work also provides a new approach for improving the stability and high QY of silicon-based carbon dot composites, and offers many application potentials in fields of environmental testing and chemical sensing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The project was supported by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of China (Grant No. 2020CDJGFCG006), the National Key Research and Development Program of China (Grant No. 2016YFE0125200) and NSFC 61927818. Thank you for the testing provided by the Analytical and Testing Center of Chongqing University and Beijing Zhongkebaice Technology Service Co., Ltd.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix B
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix B
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ceramint.2021.10.194.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Adsorption of tetracycline on single-walled and multi-walled carbon nanotubes as affected by aqueous solution chemistry
Environ. Toxicol. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Ji, W. Chen, J. Bi, S. Zheng, Z. Xu, D. Zhu, P.J. Alvarez, Adsorption of tetracycline on single-walled and multi-walled carbon nanotubes as affected by aqueous solution chemistry, Environ Toxicol Chem, 29 (2010) 2713-2719. https://doi.org/10.1002/etc.350.

ACS Omega

Mkhalid, Facile Synthesis of Mesoporous Ag2O-ZnO Heterojunctions for Efficient Promotion of Visible Light Photodegradation of Tetracycline, ACS Omega, 5 (2020) 33269-33279. https://doi.org/10.1021/acsomega.0c04969.
